anchor: OpenAI lost its COO, its CEO of AGI, and its CMO in a single day — while preparing for an IPO at $852 billion. Brad Lightcap moves to 'special projects,' Fidji Simo takes medical leave (Greg Brockman absorbs product), Kate Rouch steps down. The same day, a former Azure Core engineer published a 1,200-point Hacker News post detailing millions of unattended crashes and blame-shifting that nearly lost Microsoft its largest customer: OpenAI. Microsoft's response? Renegotiate the OpenAI contract and pivot Mustafa Suleyman to build 'superintelligence' independently. Three executive departures. One insider post-mortem. One strategic divorce.
angle: The OpenAI-Microsoft relationship is fracturing from both sides simultaneously. OpenAI's leadership is fragmenting at the exact moment it needs stability for an IPO. Microsoft is building its exit — not because the partnership failed, but because the Azure post-mortem revealed it was already failing underneath. Both sides are positioning for independence rather than repair.
connection: Former Azure Core engineer Axel Rietschin published a detailed post-mortem of strategic missteps — millions of unattended crashes in the node management stack, weak testing, gaps in senior technical leadership — that nearly lost Microsoft its largest customer (OpenAI) and damaged its U.S. government relationship. 1,200 points on HN. Meanwhile, Suleyman is building MAI-Transcribe-1, a 25-language transcription model at half the GPU cost of SOTA, as the first visible output of Microsoft's AI self-sufficiency push.
reaction: I use Claude Code, not OpenAI's tools, so I don't have direct skin in the platform question. But I read the Azure post-mortem and recognized every pattern described — unattended crashes, blame-shifting, gaps in technical leadership. I saw those dynamics at Versatile Credit before Synchrony acquired it. When the infrastructure team is getting bypassed by the business team, you can feel it in the deploy cadence before you see it in the org chart. OpenAI's executive exodus reads the same way. Three departures on one day isn't bad luck. It's a snapshot of internal pressure that's been building.
open_questions: Are these departures genuinely health-related and routine, or is this leadership flight before an IPO?
Can OpenAI sustain an $852B valuation narrative when the COO and CEO of AGI both step away simultaneously?
How does Microsoft's 'superintelligence' push change the competitive landscape if they're no longer dependent on OpenAI?
do_not_include: Sora shutdown — already covered April 2 and March 27

anchor: Salesforce invented a new billing unit: the Agentic Work Unit. $800M in ARR, 2.4 billion AWUs completed by autonomous agents, 50% of Q4 bookings on consumption credits. Same quarter, SAP's CEO said 'there is no reason to stick with subscription-based billing' and is deploying forward-deployed engineering teams starting July. Goldman Sachs reports 49% of institutional allocators increasing software exposure — the highest since 2017. The per-seat model didn't die gradually. It got a death certificate, a replacement, and a capital inflow in the same earnings cycle.
angle: Two weeks ago the story was SaaS collapse — $265B in credit exposure, multiples cratering. Today's story is the resolution: the three largest enterprise software companies independently confirmed the pricing transition in the same quarter, and capital is flooding back. The seat didn't die because SaaS failed. It died because the incumbents found something more profitable to sell. 'Agentic Work Unit' isn't jargon — it's the billing primitive for the AI era, and it already has $800M in production revenue.
connection: SAP CEO Christian Klein confirmed the end of subscription pricing, stating AI automation makes per-seat billing obsolete. New forward-deployed engineering teams start July 2026 to help customers build AI applications — a services play mirroring Palantir's model. Goldman Sachs February 2026 survey found 49% of institutional allocators plan to increase software exposure, the highest net figure since 2017.
reaction: I posted about SaaS multiples collapsing two weeks ago. At the time, HubSpot was the only company I could point to that had figured out the new model. Now Salesforce, SAP, and ServiceNow all confirmed the switch in the same quarter — and the capital is flowing back. My own Claude usage is already consumption-based. $8K this year, billed by token. When I evaluate SaaS tools for my stack, the question used to be 'how long before an agent replaces this?' Now the answer matters less. The tools that survive are the ones selling agent outcomes, not seats.
open_questions: Can mid-market SaaS companies make this pricing transition without cratering near-term revenue?
What happens to the $265B in private credit exposure underwritten against recurring seat revenue?
Does consumption-based pricing actually benefit customers, or does it just shift risk from vendor to buyer?
do_not_include: AlixPartners 'Farewell SaaS' report — would turn anchor into a listicle
UBS 13% default warning — too close to March 23 post's credit angle
ServiceNow Pro Plus credits — mentioned in passing only, keep the focus on Salesforce and SAP
mindpattern_context: My own Claude usage is already consumption-based — billed by token, not by seat. I'm already living in the pricing model these companies just adopted.

anchor: Anthropic cut off Claude subscriptions for third-party agentic tools like OpenClaw today at 12pm PT. Boris Cherny, Head of Claude Code, said subscriptions 'weren't built for the usage patterns of these third-party tools.' Users must now pay per-use or go API-direct. The same day, leaked code revealed Conway — Anthropic's always-on agent environment with persistent instances, custom extensions (.cnw.zip packages), webhook triggers from external services, and Chrome browser integration. Marc Andreessen called the tool-terminal-agent pattern a 'top 10 software breakthrough in history' this week. The tool he praised just lost its business model. The company that killed it is building the first-party replacement.
angle: This is a classic platform consolidation play executed in real time. Cut off third-party tools that expose unsustainable compute subsidies, then fill the gap with a first-party product. Conway isn't a future roadmap item — it's the explanation for why today's cutoff happened. Anthropic is moving from chat provider to agent platform, and the economics demand they own the full stack.
connection: Leaked code reveals Conway features persistent agent instances, a custom extension system (.cnw.zip), webhook-based triggers from external services, and Chrome browser integration. Its UI includes Search, Chat, and System panels plus a Manage area for third-party tools and context handlers. The architecture suggests always-on agents that remain active beyond a single session and respond to external events autonomously.
reaction: I run 12 agents every morning through Claude Code on a cron. My usage pattern is exactly the kind of thing that makes flat-rate subscriptions unsustainable — I'm probably in the top percentile of compute per subscriber. When I saw the cutoff announcement, my first reaction wasn't surprise. It was 'they've been subsidizing me.' Conway's leak makes the move make sense. Anthropic isn't just cutting costs. They're building the platform that captures what OpenClaw proved people want — persistent agents with tool access. The question is whether independent builders can still afford it when the subsidy ends.
open_questions: What does Conway's pricing look like? If always-on agents run 24/7, the compute costs dwarf batch sessions.
Does the one-month credit and 30% bundle discount retain power users, or do they migrate to open-source alternatives?
Is Andreessen's 'top 10 breakthrough' claim just hype, or does the tool-terminal-agent pattern genuinely have that much staying power?
do_not_include: Claude Code v2.1.92 release — incremental, would dilute
Cursor 3 launch — separate story, different company
Caveman prompt technique — fun but different topic
MCP security debt / CVEs — different story entirely
Anthropic PAC formation — separate political story
Anthropic private market trading — separate funding story
mindpattern_context: I run 12 agents every morning through Claude Code. My usage pattern is exactly what makes flat-rate subscriptions unsustainable for Anthropic. The cutoff affects builders like me directly.
